instructions: This is a non-exhaustive mechanism library, not a fixed category list.
Use the examples as patterns to adapt, not as categories to copy.
Invent fresh categories that follow these mechanisms or similarly fair mechanisms.

families.name: Plain semantic sets
families.description: A clean class of things most players can recognize.
examples: pasta shapes such as PENNE, FUSILLI, ORZO, RAVIOLI
musical instruments such as VIOLIN, FLUTE, DRUM, TRUMPET
avoid: using very common sets as blue or purple
repeating familiar starter sets like planets, flowers, zoo animals, or desk items

families.name: Shared hidden properties
families.description: Words share a property that is fair but not the first meaning players notice.
examples: things with keys such as PIANO, LOCK, MAP, KEYBOARD
things that can be rolled such as DICE, DOUGH, EYES, SLEEVES
avoid: properties so broad that almost anything could fit
one-off technical fits where the four words do not share the same natural phrase frame
using more than one Things you can X or Things that can be X group in the same board

families.name: Phrase completion
families.description: Each answer combines with the same word before or after it.
examples: words before BOARD, such as DASH, SURF, SCORE, KEY
words after ICE, such as CREAM, CUBE, WATER, SKATE
avoid: more than one phrase-completion group in one puzzle
using phrase completion as the default purple mechanism

families.name: Sound patterns
families.description: Words are connected by pronunciation rather than meaning.
examples: homophones of letters such as ARE, BEE, SEA, WHY
words that rhyme with numbers such as SUN, FEW, HEAVEN, DINE
avoid: near-rhymes that players would argue about
pronunciations that only work in one uncommon accent

families.name: Spelling and letter patterns
families.description: The written form hides the connection.
examples: silent first letters such as KNIFE, GNOME, WRIST, PSALM
palindromes such as LEVEL, CIVIC, KAYAK, ROTOR
avoid: anagrams unless every displayed word is common and the mapping is exact

families.name: Second identities
families.description: Common words also belong to another recognizable system.
examples: common words that are also programming languages, such as PYTHON, RUBY, SWIFT, RUST
keyboard keys that are also normal words, such as SHIFT, SPACE, RETURN, ESCAPE
avoid: systems that most players would not know
brand lists that feel like trivia
plain synonym groups as purple unless there is a second identity or another real trick

families.name: Word transformations
families.description: A small fair operation reveals the connection.
examples: remove one letter to make a new common word
add the same letter to each answer to make a new word
avoid: operations that require explaining too much after the reveal

families.name: Light trivia and culture
families.description: A familiar cultural set that does not require specialist knowledge.
examples: chess pieces such as PAWN, ROOK, BISHOP, KNIGHT
zodiac signs such as ARIES, LEO, LIBRA, PISCES
